raw_response: {"rating": -1.8, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report presenting data on abortion rates following the overturning of Roe v. Wade. The author's own prose is largely factual and data-driven, but several framing choices reflect a lean-left orientation by consistently presenting abortion access as resilient and emphasizing the ineffectiveness of state bans. The rating reflects a Lean Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The framing of the data throughout emphasizes that bans have failed to reduce abortion numbers — the author's own language highlights increases in 'all but three' ban states, consistently framing bans as ineffective rather than presenting the policy rationale or supporter perspective on the laws; (2) Shield laws and mail-order abortion pills are described matter-of-factly and without any negative framing, while the bans themselves are implicitly cast as obstacles women are successfully circumventing — a framing that aligns with pro-abortion-access advocacy; (3) The sole expert sourced by the author is a law professor described as co-author of a book titled 'After Dobbs: How the Supreme Court Ended Roe but Not Abortion' — a title with an inherently pro-access framing — and no source representing the pro-ban perspective is included or quoted anywhere in the piece; (4) The graphic header 'Abortions in States With Bans' and the leadin language 'More women from states with bans obtained abortions' consistently frame the story around the failure of bans rather than the policy debate itself."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report presenting data on abortion rates following the overturning of Roe v. Wade. The author's own prose is largely factual and data-driven, but several framing choices reflect a lean-left orientation by consistently presenting abortion access as resilient and emphasizing the ineffectiveness of state bans. The rating reflects a Lean Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The framing of the data throughout emphasizes that bans have failed to reduce abortion numbers — the author's own language highlights increases in 'all but three' ban states, consistently framing bans as ineffective rather than presenting the policy rationale or supporter perspective on the laws; (2) Shield laws and mail-order abortion pills are described matter-of-factly and without any negative framing, while the bans themselves are implicitly cast as obstacles women are successfully circumventing — a framing that aligns with pro-abortion-access advocacy; (3) The sole expert sourced by the author is a law professor described as co-author of a book titled 'After Dobbs: How the Supreme Court Ended Roe but Not Abortion' — a title with an inherently pro-access framing — and no source representing the pro-ban perspective is included or quoted anywhere in the piece; (4) The graphic header 'Abortions in States With Bans' and the leadin language 'More women from states with bans obtained abortions' consistently frame the story around the failure of bans rather than the policy debate itself.

explanation: This is a news report presenting data on abortion rates following the overturning of Roe v. Wade. The author's own prose is largely factual and data-driven, but several framing choices reflect a lean-left orientation by consistently presenting abortion access as resilient and emphasizing the ineffectiveness of state bans. The rating reflects a Lean Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The framing of the data throughout emphasizes that bans have failed to reduce abortion numbers — the author's own language highlights increases in 'all but three' ban states, consistently framing bans as ineffective rather than presenting the policy rationale or supporter perspective on the laws; (2) Shield laws and mail-order abortion pills are described matter-of-factly and without any negative framing, while the bans themselves are implicitly cast as obstacles women are successfully circumventing — a framing that aligns with pro-abortion-access advocacy; (3) The sole expert sourced by the author is a law professor described as co-author of a book titled 'After Dobbs: How the Supreme Court Ended Roe but Not Abortion' — a title with an inherently pro-access framing — and no source representing the pro-ban perspective is included or quoted anywhere in the piece; (4) The graphic header 'Abortions in States With Bans' and the leadin language 'More women from states with bans obtained abortions' consistently frame the story around the failure of bans rather than the policy debate itself.
